what_it_does: Defines a single configuration dial, Anticipation, that describes how far ahead a resolver/brain pre-fetches or pre-resolves memory and context, with three qualitative positions (reactive, warm, hot) and a default of warm.
observations: The file contains only a module docstring and a DIAL dictionary.
DIAL has keys id, label, governs, positions, and default.
The governs field is a prose string that says the dial controls pre-retrieval and names future consumers: the now-organ and resolver when built (GC14/Track-1).
It explicitly notes that nothing reads this dial yet; it is a configuration seam adjustable from day one.
positions is an ordered list of three name/meaning pairs: reactive, warm, hot.
No executable functions, classes, or imports are present.
standouts: The governs string is unusually candid about future consumers and the fact that the dial has no current readers.
It frames anticipation as a spectrum from on-demand retrieval to speculative overnight warming.
The default warm sits between reactive and hot, suggesting a deliberate balance between idle compute and foresight.
conventions: Module-level DIAL dict as the primary export.
Docstring gives file-level purpose and references Track-1 round 2.
Human-readable governs prose rather than structured metadata.
Positions expressed as ordered name/meaning pairs.
Default specified by string value matching a position name.
concerns: governs mixes design intent, consumer names, and caveats in one long string instead of structured fields.
No validation that default matches one of the position names.
Position meanings are qualitative and leave implementation thresholds undefined.
The phrase CONSUMERS (named honestly) signals the dial is a placeholder not yet wired to any code.
No schema or typing for the dial structure.
notes: This file is a design-time configuration knob for future pre-retrieval behavior, currently unconnected to any runtime consumer.
questions: How will the dial be read and enforced at runtime?
What exactly are the now-organ and resolver, and where will they live?
What events constitute a now shift that triggers warm pre-resolution?
What does hot speculation look like in practice, e.g., scheduling, cost limits, and staging storage?
Is there a plan to validate defaults and positions programmatically?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to define an anticipation dial for pre-retrieval, and that is exactly what the file does.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to create an early, adjustable policy knob for how much lookahead a future resolver should perform, so the system can be tuned before the resolver and now-organ are implemented.
novelty: It is distinct in explicitly documenting that no consumer currently reads the dial and in naming future consumers honestly within the dial metadata.
contribution: It contributes a named, documented, three-level anticipation dial with a default, providing a configuration seam that a fused system could keep for tuning future resolver/now-organ pre-retrieval behavior.
summary_for_embedding: A Python module that defines the Anticipation dial, a three-position configuration knob (reactive/warm/hot, default warm) describing how far ahead a future resolver should pre-fetch or pre-resolve context and memory.
intention_for_embedding: To establish an early, tunable policy seam for pre-retrieval/foresight behavior before its consumers are built.
